entry.title: Variable Selection II: Lasso Theory
entry.content:
Introduction

In recent

The Lasso Model

Given this framework, we can interpret the variable / subset selection problem for the lasso as the question of whether the lasso estimate has nonzero entries in the same positions as the true regression vector .

So how exactly can the lasso problem be used for variable selection? Due to the nature of the regularization term, the Lasso objective naturally enforces sparsity of the solution : it sets some coefficients exactly to 0. In this case, we can interpret the nonzero coefficients of the solution as the selected variables. The first question to ask is how the selected subset of variables depends on the objective function. There are a few points to note about this, e.g. the norm of the regularization term and the parameter

The geometry of the objective function determines the sparsity properties of the solution; note that taking (to obtain ridge regression) does not enforce sparsity in the same way. The unit balls under this norm are uniformly convex, differently so from the unit ball of the norm. We can easily see that the unit ball is the analog of the octahedron in dimensions, which has linear facets. It’s easiest to build this geometric intuition using the Lagragian formulation of the Lasso program, which is to minimize the MSE loss subject to a constraint on the norm of If we look at contours of the loss function, their intersections with balls are more likely to be at edges / points of the octahedron, where one or more of the coefficients are zero.

The regularization parameter plays an equally important role in which variables are selected. From now on, fix . In analyzing the Lasso model, we often see plots of the fitted coefficients against the regularization parameter . As increases, more and more coefficients are set to – once a variable is set to zero for some choice of it will be set to zero for all choices of the parameter greater than Indeed, for chosen large enough, all coefficients will be set to - this is easy to see, since for $\lambda$ large enough, any increment in the solution vector will be greater than a corresponding decrease in the loss function. In practice, the parameter is chosen via cross validation. The question is then to understand how the choice of the parameter affects the variable selection procedure. Too small, and no sparsity is enforced (no variables are selected); too large, and all coefficients are selected.

Theoretical Guarantees.

Two conditions on the design matrix are important when analyzing the variable selection problem:

  • Lower Eigenvalue:
  • Mutual Incoherence

Using these two conditions, we can state the following theorem:

Theorem [Wainwright Ch. 7, Thm. 7.21]. Consider an -sparse linear regession model whose design matrix satisfies the Lower Eigenvalue and Mutual Incoherence conditions above. Then for

Examples

Extensions to M-estimators

Example: Group Lasso

Sources

  • Wainwright, High Dimensional Statistics.

What other sources on Lasso consistency?

Paper by Jason D Lee. – Variable Selection on -estimators, another by Peng Zhao, Bin Yu – On the Variable Selection Consistency of Lasso

entry.title: Variable Selection I: Overview
entry.content:
Introduction

In the first project in this blog, I’d like to give an overview of methods in feature selection, a collection of techniques that try to identify collections of variables in a statistical model which are related to the.

Such algorithms are useful for several purposes, a few of which are interpretability and parsimony. For models fit with very large numbers of features (throughout the series we’ll mainly refer to such cases where $p > n$ to be high dimensional).

In the biological setting … –polygenic hypothesis / omnigenic hypotheses

Drug / gene testing or discovery in GWAS .. expensive to test all of these options.

Overview

Variable Selection via Search

Perhaps the first types of model selection or feature selection algorithms that are proposed are search algorithms, which consider various combinations of the predictor variables. These subsets can be selected via brute force subset selection, or via simplified forward and backward subset selection.

We can measure the goodness of the subsets via the following measures:

  • Akaike Information Criterion (AIC), Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC),
  • Mallow
  • Adjusted

Variable Selection via Optimization

However, most of those procedures are too expensive to be run on high dimensional problems. There have been many more computationally attractive alternatives that have been proposed, the most prominent of which you’ve likely heard of, called the LASSO procedure.

The Lasso program seeks to minimize the objective term above; basically using optimization and the behavior of the regularization norm as a stand-in for feature selection.

There are many similar variants of this. Luckily there’s a theory that covers all of these cases, under the term -estimators.

We’ll cover:

  • Lasso
  • Group Lasso
  • General -estimators

Variable Selection via Distributional Properties

  • RATE: Variable Selection via Relative Centrality. Selects importance of variables via change in distribution omitting that variable, as measured using the Kullback-Leibler divergence.
  • Knockoffs: Variable Selection using simulated ‘knockoff’ variables, with theoretical guarantees on False Discovery Rate.

Variable Selection via Loss Function Variation

Jeremiah Zhe Liu’s paper, and the JLMR 2016 paper that it considers as well. These ideas define the variable importance via the Empirical Norm of the Gradient Function,

This is also similar to the idea used in analyzing convnets (alongside occlusion methods, etc. ) – I recall we had a similar idea like this.

Motivations

The interest in this project began during my experiences working with Lorin Crawford at Brown University, where we worked on applying feature selection methods for black box models and its application to shape analysis. I hope to cover this procedure, called RATE, in a future post. The end result is a pipeline we named SINATRA, which combines the use of informative topological transforms, and Bayesian models + RATE to study differentiating features in classes of shapes. We’ll give more details later on :)

My hope is to give some understanding behind the theoretical guarantees (if explored) for these different broad groups of methodologies, as well as give some small experiments to see under which circumstances each method works best.
